splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps Auer/Kisor deference to agency interpretations of ambiguous sentencing guidelines or criminal statutes when the agency's interpretation expands criminal liability.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts should defer to anti-defendant agency interpretations of ambiguous criminal provisions or instead apply the rule of lenity in favor of the defendant. This matters because it determines whether agencies can effectively expand criminal liability through interpretive guidance rather than formal rulemaking.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes priority over Auer/Kisor deference, preventing agencies from using delegated interpretive authority to expand criminal liability without going through proper lawmaking processes.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency deference (Auer/Kisor) takes priority over the rule of lenity, allowing agency interpretations of ambiguous guidelines to expand the scope of criminal sentencing enhancements.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Sentencing Guidelines commentary (Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2) that includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy in the definition of 'controlled substance offense' is entitled to controlling deference under Stinson/Kisor when the guideline text does not mention inchoate offenses.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether Application Note 1's inclusion of conspiracy and attempt within 'controlled substance offense' for career-offender purposes is a valid interpretation entitled to deference or an impermissible expansion of the guideline text. This affects whether defendants with prior conspiracy convictions qualify as career offenders under the Sentencing Guidelines.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is a reasonable interpretation of an ambiguous guideline provision and is entitled to controlling weight, so inchoate offenses like conspiracy qualify as 'controlled substance offenses' for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary impermissibly expands guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, so Application Note 1 impermissibly adds to the definition and is not entitled to deference, meaning conspiracy convictions do not qualify as career-offender predicates.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify) or disjunctive (any single criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether a defendant must meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1) to be disqualified from safety-valve relief, or whether possessing any one criterion is disqualifying. This directly affects whether defendants facing mandatory minimum sentences can receive lower sentences.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any single criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as functionally 'or' in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as a factor supporting compassionate release, or whether such changes are categorically excluded. This is critical for prisoners serving lengthy sentences under since-amended mandatory minimums.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot contribute to compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) when the substance is controlled under state law but not under the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the career-offender guideline's reference to 'an offense under state law' requires the underlying substance to also be federally controlled, or whether any state-controlled substance suffices. This affects career-offender designations for defendants with state drug convictions involving substances not on federal schedules.

positions.label: State-controlled substance suffices
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b) broadly defines 'controlled substance offense' to include state law offenses even if the particular substance is not controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the underlying substance is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appellate deadline applies to appeals from orders denying attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment, which permits criminal defendants to recover fees for frivolous, vexatious, or bad-faith prosecutions.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter (with a longer appeal deadline) or a criminal matter (with a shorter deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies, but at least one circuit applies the criminal deadline.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment fee motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the civil appeal deadline (typically 30 or 60 days) applies rather than the criminal deadline.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment fee motion arises in and is part of the criminal case, so the shorter criminal appeal deadline (14 days) applies.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Referenced in Onamuti as minority view

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs after a Chapter 13 filing but before conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. This affects whether trustees can sell appreciated assets to satisfy creditors after conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), read with § 541(a)'s broad definition of estate property, means that post-petition appreciation in asset value belongs to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that the Bankruptcy Code's structure, including § 348(f)(1)(A), establishes that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the debtors, not the estate.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Referenced in Castleman dissent as contrary position

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly impose sentences upon revocation of supervised release as punishment for the violation of supervised release conditions, or whether such sentences must be limited to facilitating the defendant's transition back into the community.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the permissible purposes of sentences imposed upon revocation of supervised release. The Sixth Circuit allows courts to expressly punish defendants for violations, placing it at one extreme of a circuit split, while other circuits limit revocation sentences to non-punitive purposes.

positions.label: Punishment is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release conditions when imposing revocation sentences.

positions.label: Punishment is not a permissible purpose
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should be limited to facilitating the defendant's transition back into the community and addressing the breach of trust, not expressly punishing the violation itself.
